source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Subsea Leak at MC 778 A (Thunderhorse)

context.region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 778

event.incident_type: Equipment Failure - Rod Piston Seal Failure
actions_taken: Well TD004 shut in at 1000 hours
ROV inspection conducted to identify leak source
Troubleshooting of control lines and SCSSV performed
Testing confirmed integrity of A-side and SCSSV
Notifications made to National Response Center
event.summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.
recommendations: BSEE to consider developing policy to address potential SCSSV rod piston seal failures to prevent reoccurrence
BSEE to perform detailed investigation into rod piston seal failures affecting BP's subsea wells
Follow-up meeting scheduled between BSEE and BP engineering and leadership to investigate similarities in multiple rod piston seal failures over the last 3 years

hazards.name: Pressurized hydrocarbon and hydraulic fluid in subsea well
Subsea equipment degradation and seal failure
Loss of platform power and control systems

threats.name: Rod piston seal failure in SCSSV
Hydraulic fluid ingress into well fluids
Loss of subsurface safety valve control due to power failure

controls.name: Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV)
controls.barrier_role: Primary barrier to prevent uncontrolled hydrocarbon discharge
controls.lod_basis: Subsurface safety device designed to isolate well

supporting_text: apparent failure of the Surface Control Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) in Well TD004
rod piston seals of the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve had failed, allowing oil and gas to enter the hydraulic lines
The probable cause of the leak is an equipment failure related to the rod piston seal on the SCSSV

controls.name: Subsea Leak Detection System (C-ROC Algorithm)
controls.barrier_role: Detection of hydrocarbon discharge to enable rapid response
controls.lod_basis: Algorithmic sensor-based leak detection using pressure trend analysis

supporting_text: BP's subsea leak detection system, known as Condition Rate of Change (C-ROC Algorithm), uses a conditional rate of change (an algorithmic sensor-based leak detection technique) which detects changes in pressure trends
BSEE confirmed that the system was active at the time of the leak but could not detect this relatively small discharge

controls.name: Emergency Backup Power System
controls.barrier_role: Maintain critical platform power and control system functionality during power loss
controls.lod_basis: Automatic backup power to restore critical systems

supporting_text: the emergency back-up power responded as designed and returned critical power to the platform within 45 seconds

controls.name: Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) Subsea Inspection
controls.barrier_role: Visual detection and confirmation of subsea leaks and equipment condition
controls.lod_basis: Direct visual inspection of subsea equipment and discharge

supporting_text: a subsea leak was observed by a Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) during a subsea inspection
On 11 June 2022 at 1500 hrs (NRC# 1338496), BP observed (by the ROV) a crude oil release (through bubbles) coming out the subsea control module vent

controls.name: Well Shut-in Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Isolate well and stop hydrocarbon discharge
controls.lod_basis: Operational procedure to close well and SCSSV

supporting_text: Well TD004 was shut in at 1000 hours with the SCSSV closing around 1030 hours

controls.name: Hydraulic Fluid Consumption Monitoring
controls.barrier_role: Early detection of SCSSV failure through abnormal hydraulic consumption
controls.lod_basis: Operational monitoring of hydraulic system parameters

supporting_text: During the shutdown, an increase in hydraulic fluid consumption was observed
Well TD004 was identified as the cause for the consumption and subsea leak

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
